title: User Interaction Mastery
tagline: Forms, events, and real user experiences.

accomplishments: Build a signup form that checks if inputs are valid
Create real-time validation that gives instant feedback
Stop bad data before it gets saved

do.title: Build a Simple Form Validator
steps: Try submitting with empty fields — see error messages!
do.code:
Sign Up Form

✅ Form submitted successfully!
do.result: JavaScript VALIDATED the inputs before accepting them. This prevents bad data!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing error messages for both fields when validation fails.

concepts.name: Getting input values with .value
concepts.definition: document.getElementById('field').value returns whatever the user typed. It's always a string, even for number inputs.
concepts.analogy: Reading what someone wrote on a form — .value is the answer they filled in.

concepts.name: Checking if empty
concepts.definition: if (value === "") checks if the user left the field blank. Empty string means nothing was typed.
concepts.analogy: Checking if a box is empty before shipping it.

concepts.name: Checking string length
concepts.definition: .length works on strings just like arrays. "hi".length is 2. Use it to enforce minimum/maximum character counts.
concepts.analogy: Measuring a piece of string — is it long enough for what you need?

concepts.name: .includes() for content checks
concepts.definition: string.includes('@') returns true if the string contains that character. Great for email validation.
concepts.analogy: Searching a document for a specific word — is it in there or not?

concepts.name: classList.add() and .remove()
concepts.definition: Add or remove CSS classes with JavaScript. This lets you change styling (red borders, green borders) without editing CSS.
concepts.analogy: Putting on or taking off a costume — the person (element) is the same, but they look different.

concepts.name: oninput for real-time validation
concepts.definition: The oninput event fires every time the user types a character. Use it for live feedback like password strength checkers.
concepts.analogy: A spell-checker that underlines words as you type, not after you finish.

understand.keyTakeaway: Good validation: (1) Get the value, (2) Check if it's valid, (3) Give helpful feedback. Use .value, .length, .includes(), and classList.

build.goal: Build a password strength checker with real-time feedback
build.hint: oninput fires on EVERY keystroke. That's why it updates instantly as you type!

challenge.title: Build a Complete Registration Form
challenge.mission: Create a full registration form that validates username, email, password, confirm password, and age.
requirements: Show specific error messages for each failure
Green borders for valid, red for invalid
challenge.starterCode: <!-- Build a complete registration form with 5 validated fields -->

capabilities: Add/remove CSS classes for visual feedback

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn how to handle EVENTS — clicks, keypresses, mouse movements, and more. You'll build interactive games and tools.

accomplishments: Detect keyboard presses and respond to them
Build a game you control with arrow keys
Create interactive experiences that feel alive

do.title: Build a Keyboard-Controlled Box
steps: Click anywhere on the page first (to give it focus)
Press the arrow keys — watch the red box move!
do.code:
🎮 Keyboard Controller

Use Arrow Keys to move the red box
X: 375, Y: 225
do.result: JavaScript listened for keyboard events and moved the box based on which arrow key you pressed!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the box moved to a different position.

concepts.name: What is an event?
concepts.definition: Something that happens in the browser — a user action (click, keypress, mouse move) or browser action (page loaded). JavaScript can listen for these and respond.
concepts.analogy: A motion sensor — it detects when something happens and triggers a response.

concepts.name: addEventListener() vs onclick
concepts.definition: addEventListener('click', function) is the modern way. Better than onclick because: separates HTML/JS, allows multiple listeners, gives you the event object, can be removed.
concepts.analogy: onclick is a sticky note on one button. addEventListener is a proper notification system.

concepts.name: The event object
concepts.definition: The function you pass to addEventListener receives an event object with info about what happened: event.key (which key), event.type (what kind), event.target (which element).
concepts.analogy: A detective report — it tells you what happened, where, when, and how.

concepts.name: Keyboard events: keydown and keyup
concepts.definition: keydown fires when a key is pressed. keyup fires when released. event.key gives the key name: 'ArrowUp', 'Enter', 'a', ' ' (space).
concepts.analogy: keydown = pressing a piano key. keyup = releasing it.

concepts.name: Browser coordinate system
concepts.definition: X increases left to right. Y increases top to bottom (opposite of math class!). (0,0) is the top-left corner.
concepts.analogy: Reading a page — you start at the top-left and go right and down.

concepts.name: setInterval() for timed events
concepts.definition: setInterval(function, milliseconds) runs code repeatedly at a set interval. clearInterval(timer) stops it. 1000ms = 1 second.
concepts.analogy: An alarm clock that goes off every X seconds until you turn it off.

concepts.name: Creating elements dynamically
concepts.definition: document.createElement('div') creates a new element. Set its properties, then element.appendChild(newElement) adds it to the page.
concepts.analogy: Building a Lego piece (create), decorating it (set properties), then snapping it onto your model (appendChild).

understand.keyTakeaway: addEventListener() is the modern way to handle events. The event object tells you everything about what happened.

build.goal: Build a Click Speed Game with targets, score, and countdown timer

codeComparison.old:
// Old way: onclick in HTML
<button onclick="doSomething()">Click</button>

build.hint: setInterval() keeps running until you stop it with clearInterval(). That's how the countdown works!

challenge.title: Build a Keyboard Piano
challenge.mission: Create a virtual piano where pressing A, S, D, F, G, H, J lights up corresponding piano keys.
requirements: Each responds to a keyboard letter (A-J)
Keys light up on keydown, return to normal on keyup
Display which note is being played
Support both keyboard and mouse click
challenge.starterCode:
<!-- Build a piano with .key elements using data-key attributes -->
<!-- Listen for keydown to add "active" class -->
<!-- Listen for keyup to remove "active" class -->
<!-- Use document.querySelector(`.key[data-key="${key}"]`) to find matching key -->
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add actual sound using the Web Audio API or HTML5 <audio> elements!

capabilities: Use addEventListener() to handle events properly
Detect keyboard presses with keydown and keyup
Access event info through the event object
Use data attributes to connect HTML and JavaScript

bridge.preview: Fantastic! You can now handle all kinds of user interactions. In the next lesson, you'll learn complex conditionals, switch statements, and smart decision-making in your code.
